era_context: Late 16th century: The Protestant Reformation and Catholic Counter-Reformation are reshaping European religious and political boundaries. The Spanish Habsburg Empire under Philip II is at the height of its power. The Ottoman Empire remains a significant Mediterranean and Eastern European force. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty rules China; Japan is in the Sengoku period of fragmentation. The Age of Exploration is accelerating, with European colonial ventures expanding in the Americas, Africa, and Asia.

events.title: Drake's circumnavigation expedition departs England
events.description: Sir Francis Drake departed Plymouth, England on 13 December 1577 aboard the Golden Hinde with a fleet of five ships, beginning his circumnavigation of the globe. The expedition was ostensibly a trading voyage but was actually a privateering mission authorized by Queen Elizabeth I to raid Spanish settlements and treasure ships in the Pacific and Atlantic. Drake's voyage would last until 1579 and would be the second circumnavigation in history and the first by an English captain. The expedition represented English naval ambition and contributed to growing tensions with Spain.
key_figures: Philip II of Spain (target of raids)

sources.name: English court records and contemporary chronicles
Drake's voyage records and later accounts

events.certainty_note: The departure date of 13 December 1577 is well-documented in contemporary English records and multiple historical sources.

events.title: Oda Nobunaga consolidates control of central Japan
events.description: Oda Nobunaga, the dominant military figure in Japan, continued his consolidation of power in 1577 and the following years. By 1577, Nobunaga had brought much of central and western Japan under his control through military campaigns and strategic alliances. His campaign to unify Japan was ongoing, and he would continue to expand his territory until his death in 1582. The period 1577–1582 represents the final phase of Nobunaga's unification efforts before his assassination.

sources.name: Japanese chronicles and records of the Sengoku period
Historiographic consensus on Nobunaga's unification campaigns

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Nobunaga's consolidation during the late 1570s is well-documented, though specific events within 1577 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Jesuit missions expand in the Philippines
events.description: Jesuit missionaries, who had arrived in the Philippines in 1581 (note: this is slightly after 1577, but the Spanish colonization of the Philippines began in 1565), were beginning to establish a presence in the islands during the late 1570s. Spanish colonial expansion in the Philippines was accelerating, and religious conversion was a key component of Spanish colonial policy. By 1577, Spanish settlements were being established in the Philippines, and missionary activity was beginning, though the major Jesuit expansion would occur in the 1580s and beyond.

sources.name: Spanish colonial records and missionary accounts
Historiographic consensus on Spanish colonization of the Philippines

events.certainty_note: The exact timeline of Jesuit arrival in the Philippines is debated; major Jesuit expansion occurred in the 1580s, but Spanish colonial presence was established in the 1560s–1570s.

events.title: Wanli Emperor's early reign in Ming China
events.description: The Wanli Emperor (Zhu Yijun), who had ascended to the throne in 1572 as a child, was in the early years of his reign in 1577. He was under the regency and tutelage of Zhang Juzheng, a powerful eunuch and administrator who effectively ruled China during the 1570s and early 1580s. The Wanli reign (1572–1620) was marked by significant cultural and economic development, but also by increasing fiscal strain and military pressures on the northern frontier. Zhang Juzheng's administrative reforms during this period were significant for Ming governance.

sources.name: Ming court records and official histories
Historiographic consensus on Wanli reign chronology

events.certainty_note: The general context of the Wanli Emperor's early reign and Zhang Juzheng's regency is well-documented, though specific events within 1577 are less prominently recorded.

events.title: Ottoman-Safavid War begins
events.description: The Ottoman-Safavid War (1578–1590), also known as the War of the Three Whales, began in 1577–1578 with Ottoman military campaigns against the Safavid Persian Empire. The war was fought primarily in the Caucasus and the Caspian region, with the Ottomans seeking to expand their territory and influence at the expense of the Safavids. The war resulted in significant Ottoman territorial gains, including control of Georgia and parts of the Caucasus. The conflict was part of the broader Ottoman-Safavid rivalry that had characterized the 16th century.
events.certainty_note: The Ottoman-Safavid War is dated to 1578–1590 in most sources, though some sources place the beginning in 1577. The exact starting date is slightly uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: Drake's circumnavigation is sometimes portrayed as a direct cause of Spanish-English tensions, but the relationship was more complex; tensions had been building for years, and Drake's voyage was one of many provocations. The characterization of the Wanli Emperor's reign as entirely under Zhang Juzheng's control is somewhat oversimplified; the emperor had some agency even as a child, and the relationship between emperor and regent was more nuanced than simple domination. The Ottoman-Safavid War's starting date is sometimes given as 1578 rather than 1577, reflecting uncertainty in the sources about when the conflict formally began.
historiographic_note: The year 1577 is well-documented for European and East Asian events, with abundant primary sources (diplomatic correspondence, military records, chronicles). Drake's departure is particularly well-documented due to its significance for English naval history. European sources dominate the historical record; events in East Asia, Africa, and the Americas are less densely documented in Western sources. The Ottoman-Safavid conflict is documented in Ottoman and Persian sources but is less prominent in Western historiography. The Wanli Emperor's early reign is well-documented in Chinese sources but less so in Western historical accounts.

graph_edges.note: Drake's departure in 1577 led to his circumnavigation voyage, which returned to England in 1579.
Nobunaga's consolidation of power in 1577 continued until his death in 1582.
Spanish colonial expansion in the Philippines in the late 1570s led to the arrival of Jesuit missionaries in 1581.
The Wanli Emperor's early reign in 1577 was part of the broader period of Zhang Juzheng's regency (1572–1582).
The Ottoman-Safavid War beginning in 1577–1578 lasted until 1590.
